source.title: Global Sukuk Markets Reach $718 Billion as Sovereign Issuers Drive Islamic Capital Market Renaissance
source.summary: Market Resilience Amid Global Economic HeadwindsThe global sukuk market has demonstrated remarkable resilience in 2024, with outstanding issuances reaching approximately $718 billion despite...
source.author: Editorial Desk
source.body_text: The global sukuk market has demonstrated remarkable resilience in 2024, with outstanding issuances reaching approximately $718 billion despite persistent macroeconomic challenges including elevated interest rates and geopolitical tensions. According to data from the Islamic Financial Services Board (IFSB), total sukuk issuance for the year is projected to exceed $185 billion, representing a modest 3.2% increase from 2023 and signaling sustained confidence in Shariah-compliant debt instruments. The market's composition reveals a strategic shift in issuer profiles. Sovereign and quasi-sovereign entities now account for 68% of total issuances, with Malaysia maintaining its position as the world's largest sukuk market with $385 billion in outstanding instruments. Saudi Arabia has emerged as the fastest-growing market, with the Kingdom's Public Investment Fund and Saudi Aramco collectively issuing $28 billion in sukuk during 2024, marking a 42% year-on-year increase. Indonesia's sovereign sukuk program, which includes innovative green and social sukuk structures, contributed $15 billion to global volumes. This concentration of sovereign issuance reflects both opportunity and challenge. While government backing provides stability and benchmark pricing for the broader market, the relative scarcity of corporate sukuk (particularly from non-financial institutions) remains a structural constraint limiting market depth and diversification. The Islamic capital markets have witnessed significant product innovation, moving beyond traditional asset-backed structures toward more sophisticated instruments. Green sukuk have emerged as a particularly dynamic segment, with cumulative issuances surpassing $35 billion globally. The International Finance Corporation's $1.5 billion green sukuk issued in October 2024 established new standards for environmental, social, and governance (ESG) integration within Shariah-compliant frameworks. This convergence of Islamic finance and sustainable investing principles finds natural theological grounding. The Quranic concept of stewardship (khilafah) and the prohibition against harm (la darar wa la dirar) align seamlessly with contemporary ESG objectives. As Allah states in Surah Al-A'raf: "And cause not corruption upon the earth after its reformation" (7:56). This principle has enabled Islamic financial institutions to position themselves…
